content: I want to learn about reggae bass playing. What are the foundational concepts I should start with? Specifically the difference between 'one drop' and 'rockers' style, and how the bass interacts with the kick drum in each. I'm coming from a rock background where the bass and kick usually lock on every quarter note, so the syncopation feels foreign.

Great starting question. The two patterns differ in where they place emphasis. In 'one drop', the kick and snare both hit on beat 3, leaving beats 1, 2, and 4 unaccented from the drum kit. The bass typically lays out on beat 1 and plays a syncopated line that lands on 3 with the kick. In 'rockers', the kick plays all four quarter notes, more like rock, so the bass has more freedom to play melodic lines around it. The feel of one-drop is wide and spacious; rockers is more driving and dense.

That makes sense. Now, when I'm practicing this, how do I internalize the 'space' in one-drop? My instinct is always to fill it. Are there exercises specifically for learning to LEAVE space? I find myself adding ghost notes or sliding into beat 3 instead of waiting.

The fill-everything instinct is the universal trap for rock players moving into reggae. Two exercises help. First, play with a metronome on beats 2 and 4 only (set it to half-time and offset). Just play a single note on beat 3, repeated, for 5 minutes. Resist any embellishment. Second, listen to Aston 'Family Man' Barrett bass lines and try to play ONLY the notes he plays, with the same duration. He rests more than he plays. The discipline is the technique. Avoid the temptation to slide; the rest IS the part.

Let's shift topics. I have a BOSS TU-3 tuner pedal that I want to use as the head of a pedal chain. It has a DC OUT jack on the side. Can I use that to power other pedals daisy-chained off it? What are the practical limits — voltage, current draw, polarity?

Yes, the TU-3's DC OUT jack is designed exactly for daisy-chaining. It outputs 9V DC, center-negative polarity (the standard BOSS convention), with a maximum current of 200mA. That 200mA is the limit you have to budget against. A typical analog overdrive draws around 10mA, a digital delay 50-100mA, a digital reverb 100mA+. You can comfortably power 3-4 analog pedals or 1-2 digital ones off the TU-3. Beyond that you'll get noise or pedals will start dropping out as voltage sags.

Okay so for a chain of tuner -> overdrive -> chorus -> delay, where the delay is digital and draws say 80mA, plus the overdrive at 10mA and chorus at 15mA, that's 105mA total drawn from the TU-3. Well within 200mA. The TU-3 itself needs power too — does the input adapter need to account for both the TU-3 AND everything daisy-chained?

Correct calculation: 105mA of downstream load is safe. And yes, the upstream adapter must supply the TU-3's own draw PLUS everything daisy-chained. The TU-3 draws about 55mA on its own. So your adapter needs to deliver 55mA + 105mA = 160mA minimum. The stock BOSS PSA-120S adapter supplies 500mA at 9V center-negative, so you have headroom. If you were running a 200mA adapter (some old ones), you'd be at the edge.

Switching topics again. I want to set up a simple home recording rig for bass DI plus a single mic on a small amp. What's the minimum I need in terms of interface? I'm on macOS, using Logic Pro. Don't need anything fancy, just clean signal to two inputs.

For two-input recording on macOS with Logic, a 2-channel USB interface is the floor. Look for: two combo XLR/TRS jacks (so you can take bass DI on one and mic on the other), phantom power (48V) on the mic channel for condensers, at least 24-bit/48kHz converters, and direct monitoring (zero-latency headphone out). Focusrite Scarlett 2i2 (4th gen) is the safe default — about $200, class-compliant on Mac so no drivers needed, decent preamps for the price. Audient EVO 8 and PreSonus Studio 26c are reasonable alternatives. Avoid anything that requires installing drivers on modern macOS.
